variables.N: Total number of slits in the grating

locked_facts: Principal maxima condition: d sinθ = nλ, where n = 0, ±1, ±2, …
Resolving power: R = nN, where n is the order and N is the total number of slits
Grating element d = 1/N_per_unit_length (e.g., 5000 lines/cm → d = 2 × 10⁻⁴ cm)
Angular dispersion: dθ/dλ = n/(d cosθ) — higher orders are more dispersed
Maximum order observable: n_max = d/λ (since sinθ ≤ 1)
Principal maxima are extremely sharp and narrow compared to double slit fringes — sharpness increases with N
truth_statements: A diffraction grating produces very sharp, widely separated principal maxima because N slits contribute constructively only at precise angles satisfying d sinθ = nλ
The resolving power R = nN determines the ability to distinguish two closely spaced wavelengths — more slits and higher order both improve resolution
Between adjacent principal maxima there are (N − 1) minima and (N − 2) secondary maxima, which are negligibly faint for large N
The angular dispersion dθ/dλ = n/(d cosθ) increases with order n and is independent of the number of slits N
A grating can separate white light into its component wavelengths — each order produces a complete spectrum (violet closest to centre, red furthest)

aha_moment.what_to_show: Compare double slit (N=2) with N=5, N=50, N=500. As N increases, the principal maxima become razor-sharp while the same condition d sinθ = nλ holds. The secondary maxima between principal maxima become negligible. A grating is just many slits cooperating to make the pattern ultra-precise.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Animated intensity plot morphing from broad double-slit fringes to needle-sharp grating peaks as N increases from 2 to 500
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that a grating is not a fundamentally different device — it is the logical extension of the double slit, where more slits = sharper peaks

simulation_states.label: Light hits the grating
simulation_states.description: Monochromatic light approaches a grating with many equally spaced slits. The grating element d and number of slits N are labelled. The screen is placed at a large distance.
simulation_states.emphasis: A diffraction grating is essentially a large number of equally spaced parallel slits

simulation_states.label: Waves from all slits superpose
simulation_states.description: Each slit sends a wavelet toward the screen. At certain angles, all N wavelets arrive in phase (path difference between adjacent slits = nλ). At other angles, they cancel out almost completely.
simulation_states.emphasis: Constructive interference from N slits simultaneously requires d sinθ = nλ exactly — even a tiny deviation causes massive cancellation

simulation_states.label: Sharp principal maxima
simulation_states.description: The screen shows extremely sharp bright lines at angles satisfying d sinθ = nλ (n = 0, ±1, ±2). Between them, the intensity is essentially zero. Compared to a double slit pattern (shown faintly), the grating peaks are much narrower and brighter.
simulation_states.emphasis: Sharpness comes from N slits — more slits = sharper peaks. Resolving power R = nN.

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts the number of slits N, grating spacing d, and wavelength λ. Increasing N sharpens the peaks dramatically. Using white light separates colours in each order. Two close wavelengths can be resolved when R = nN is large enough.
simulation_states.emphasis: More slits = sharper lines. Try white light to see spectral separation. Can you resolve sodium doublet?

common_misconceptions.belief: A diffraction grating works on a completely different principle from Young's double slit
trigger_phrases: grating aur slit mein koi connection nahi
common_misconceptions.correction: A diffraction grating works on exactly the same principle as a double slit — multi-slit interference. The condition d sinθ = nλ is the same. The only difference is the number of slits N: more slits make the peaks sharper and brighter, but the physics is identical.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate N increasing from 2 (double slit) to 500 (grating) showing the same peak positions but dramatically sharpening peaks

common_misconceptions.belief: Higher order means brighter maxima in a grating
common_misconceptions.correction: Higher order maxima are generally LESS intense, not more. The single-slit diffraction envelope of each individual slit modulates the multi-slit pattern, causing intensity to fall off at larger angles. The zeroth order (n=0) is typically the brightest.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Overlay the single-slit diffraction envelope on the grating pattern, showing how it suppresses higher-order peaks

legend.meaning: Grating element — distance between adjacent slits (m)
Total number of slits in the grating
